straight_beat.name: Francis 'Frankie' Sheehan
straight_beat.column:
The Sox lost again. That’s the box, bub. Not that it matters, but the Cardinals’ bats found some rhythm yesterday, and Boston’s pitching staff looked like a group of guys who’d rather be somewhere else. You can’t blame the weather, or the umpires, or the fact that the team’s still in a funk. You can’t even blame the fact that they’re 4-9 and four games back in the AL East. That’s the box, bub. You just have to accept it and move on.  

But here’s the thing: this isn’t the first time the Sox have been in this position. Back in 2004, they were 3-5 in April and still went on to win the World Series. So maybe there’s hope. Or maybe not. The difference then was that they had a team that could string together hits, steal bases, and hit home runs when they

optimist.name: Maureen 'Mo' Kilbane
optimist.column:
The Red Sox lost yesterday, sure, but let’s not forget what’s in the oven. You know, the real recipe for a playoff run? Patience. A little bit of that Southie grit, and a whole lot of faith in the kids. Triston Casas is a goddamn monument waiting to happen. By July that lumber is going into the Mass Pike, I swear on my grandmother’s cannoli. You think he’s just a rookie? He’s a rookie with a fastball that’ll make you forget the last three years of your life.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Mo, the team’s 4-9. How’s that a recipe for success?” Well, let’s talk about the bullpen. That’s

pessimist.name: Artie Gavrilov
pessimist.column:
The Red Sox lost again. Another game, another loss, another reminder that this team is stuck in a loop of mediocrity. Boston 2, St. Louis 3. The Cardinals didn’t just beat us—they embarrassed us with their patience, their hustle, their ability to make the most of their opportunities. Meanwhile, we’re still trying to figure out why we’re still here.  

This isn’t just about a bad game. It’s about a season that feels like it’s been delayed by a decade. The AL East is a gauntlet, sure, but even the Mets and Yankees have figured out how to win. We’re still clinging to the hope that someone in Pawtucket will suddenly become a .260 hitter, or that some trade deadline panic will fix what’s broken. But here’s the thing: the front office hasn’t done a damn thing to fix it.  

They traded Mookie Betts. That’s the starting point. Not just a trade—this was a betrayal. Betts was a cornerstone, a guy who could carry a team. Now he’s in LA, hitting walk-offs and making heroes out of strangers, while we’re stuck with a lineup that looks like it was assembled in the 1980s. The numbers don’t lie: our offense is a mess. We’re scoring fewer runs than we did last year, and our payroll is higher. That’s not a coincidence. That
